title: TBM 391: You’re Not Misaligned. You’re Thinking Differently.
questions: Why do my teammates disagree even when we share the same intent and desired outcomes?
How can I tell whether the conflict is about thinking style (abstract vs concrete) rather than strategy or trust?
What should a facilitator do when participants need artifacts but others are still in exploratory mode?
How do we collaborate effectively when people have different windows for focus and different needs for examples?
search_terms: how to communicate with concrete thinkers
how to communicate with abstract thinkers
are we misaligned or just thinking differently
summary: The post argues that many workplace conflicts labeled as misalignment are actually clashes between abstract (purpose/pattern) and concrete (steps/artifacts) thinking styles. It’s aimed at leaders, facilitators, and product/strategy practitioners who need to collaborate effectively under uncertainty and stress. The core takeaway is to treat communication as “different cognitive languages” rather than a battle of right vs wrong.

title: TBM 337: Pirates and Garbage Cans
questions: Why do conflicts and unresolved problems linger so long inside companies?
When should teams escalate or seek outside help instead of trying to solve issues locally?
How do power dynamics and accountability boundaries affect whether people surface conflicts honestly?
What organizational norms make conflict resolution acceptable, and who do those norms serve or harm?
search_terms: organizational decision-making ambiguity problems solutions actors
conflict avoidance turnover inefficiency passive-aggressive workarounds
pirate ship governance analogy conflict resolution
facilitation course internal facilitators team dynamics
summary: The post explores why organizational conflicts and problems persist for long periods, arguing that business decision-making often resembles the garbage can model rather than a rational process. It’s aimed at change agents and leaders who want practical ways to make conflict resolution more likely by adjusting culture, norms, and escalation pathways—especially when fear and identity-protection block honest dialogue.

title: systems *doing* and complexity *doing*
questions: How do we run experiments in complex environments without turning them into excuses for unilateral action?
What does it mean to “do by not doing” when we feel pressure to react immediately?
How can teams hold competing narratives and avoid premature synthesis or narrative fallacy?
When should we resist breaking a system into parts and instead work with the whole as an interacting context?
search_terms: constraint as a tool for discussion
summary: The post outlines practical behaviors for working in complex systems, emphasizing how to facilitate collaboration across perspectives while sustaining ambiguity. It’s aimed at leaders and teams who need to run experiments, avoid premature convergence, and balance simplification with fidelity to messy underlying realities.

title: TBM 313: Chat, Breathe, and Reflect
questions: How should a team change its communication patterns in a dynamic, uncertain environment?
What does it mean to reduce communication distance and increase information exchange frequency in practice?
Why do communication improvements fail when trust, psychological safety, or bandwidth are lacking?
How can an IC or non-senior team member increase sensemaking without self-gaslighting?
summary: The post argues that in dynamic, uncertain environments, effective teams must rewire communication by shortening information paths, increasing how often they exchange updates, and learning to interpret confounding inputs. It emphasizes that these changes require trust, psychological safety, cognitive flexibility, and sufficient bandwidth—otherwise they feel inefficient and can fail. It is aimed at leaders and individual contributors trying to navigate messy, high-ambiguity conditions (e.g., in tech) without losing coherence or morale.

title: TBM 283: Just and But
questions: Am I more of a juster or a butter in how I approach decisions and problems?
How does the balance of justers and butters on my team affect innovation and execution?
When has my bias toward action or questioning helped me—and when has it caused mistakes?
How does trust (or lack of it) change how justers and butters interact and collaborate?
search_terms: bias toward action vs bias toward questioning
how to balance execution and critique
building trust between different thinking styles
summary: This post argues that “just” and “but” are seductive but complementary cognitive styles: justing favors context-free action and simplification, while butting favors context-heavy questioning and exploration. It’s aimed at people leading teams or making decisions who want practical self-awareness and collaboration guidance to balance execution with critique, especially when trust is present.

title: TBM 274: How Capable Leaders Navigate Uncertainty and Ambiguity
questions: What behaviors would I observe in a capable leader navigating uncertainty and ambiguity?
How can I hire for these capabilities using interview questions and clear definitions?
How do I balance quick wins with tackling the most uncertain parts of a situation?
What does it mean to pursue coherence instead of forcing alignment in complex work?
summary: The post outlines a set of explicit, observable leadership capabilities for navigating uncertainty and ambiguity in complex environments. It is aimed at leaders and organizations that want to move beyond vague “soft skills” labels by defining behaviors, using interview questions, and recognizing that many capabilities emerge from group interactions and context. The capabilities emphasize sensemaking, safe experimentation, balancing exploration with coherence, and anticipating downstream effects.

title: TBM 273: Transition and Reflection
questions: How do we move beyond the system vs individual blame/credit loop in workplace problems?
What role do belief system clashes play in team and organizational conflict?
How can we create coherence across strategy, incentives, architecture, culture, and goals?
Should capability models come after alignment and awareness work—or can they lead?
summary: This post is a personal reflection on how to think about work, improvement, and collaboration, especially after experiencing a RIF and transitioning roles. It argues that progress in organizations depends on moving beyond the system/individual dichotomy, building self/other awareness around belief-system tensions, and seeking coherence across multiple “frames” before using the capability view to drive change. It’s aimed at leaders and practitioners who want a pragmatic, learning-oriented approach to organizational improvement.

title: TBM 269: Three Organizational Design Principles
questions: How can we design organizations so leadership collaboration matches the collaboration required at the front line?
What alignment gaps prevent teams from succeeding even when everyone is trying hard?
What happens when leadership avoids the 'elephants in the room' and teams must improvise?
How does Ashby’s law apply to organizational design and managerial decision-making?
search_terms: alignment gaps leaders vs front line
elephants in the room leadership avoidance
Ashby's law of requisite variety in organizations
human bandwidth limits for trust and teams
summary: The post proposes three organizational design principles for improving collaboration between leaders/managers and front-line teams, arguing that leadership alignment and collaboration must be at least as strong as what the work requires. It frames organizational failure modes as alignment gaps and leadership avoidance of “elephants in the room,” which push teams into fragile pragmatism. It is aimed at leaders and managers designing org structures and operating models for complex work.

title: TBM 265: Rebuilding Trust and Breaking Free From Trust Proxies and The Swirl
questions: How do we tell whether what we’re debating is a proxy for a deeper trust issue?
What causes trust spirals between a few senior leaders to paralyze an entire organization?
How can leaders improve information flow so reality can be discussed instead of politicized stand-offs?
What practical steps help teams rebuild trust without scapegoating individuals?
summary: This post explores how organizational trust problems—especially between senior leaders—create a self-reinforcing cycle where teams and leaders rely on “trust proxies” instead of addressing underlying issues. It’s aimed at leaders and managers who need practical ways to break the distrust spiral by improving information flow, labeling proxy behaviors, taking responsibility, and reframing “trust” as “tension.”

title: TBM 260: The Thoughtful HIPPO
questions: Why is it hardest to make decisions when problems are slowly getting worse?
How can leaders help teams avoid analysis paralysis and trade-off overload?
What does empowerment look like when times are deteriorating?
When should leaders use formal authority to set constraints versus letting teams decide?
summary: The post argues that top-down decision-making is especially valuable in slowly deteriorating situations where ambiguity and low pressure trigger analysis paralysis and “trade-off tetris.” It’s aimed at leaders and managers who need to set enabling constraints, reduce team cognitive load, and make accountable calls without becoming a bottleneck. The guidance emphasizes focusing on the 80/20 and using formal authority to draw the line when empowerment alone won’t work.

title: TBM 246: Why Didn't They Say No?
questions: Why would a team knowingly agree to work they can’t realistically finish?
What makes saying no so hard in organizations?
How do cognitive biases and FOMO drive overcommitment?
How does hierarchy and translation of decisions turn no into yes with tweaks?
search_terms: why teams say yes to impossible work
how to say no at work
FOMO at work and decision making
summary: The post explores why teams sometimes agree to work they cannot feasibly complete, even when the rational choice would be to refuse. It’s aimed at leaders and managers who want to understand the behavioral, political, and cognitive drivers of overcommitment so they can better support realistic scope, negotiation, and capacity decisions.

title: TBM 244: We JUST Need to Align Incentives
questions: What do people actually mean when they say “align incentives” at work?
Why do incentive changes sometimes work in one phase but fail after organizational change?
How do incentives emerge and interact in complex adaptive systems?
What alternatives exist to incentive alignment during annual planning?
summary: This post challenges the common management mantra of “align incentives” by reframing incentives as forces in complex adaptive systems that can create unintended attractors and perverse outcomes. It’s aimed at people dealing with annual planning, organizational politics, and culture dynamics who want a more systems-based way to think about incentives and control. The takeaway is to focus less on perfectly engineering incentives and more on creating conditions that enable people to shape their environment.

title: TBM 239: "Get Creative"
questions: How can I ask for creative solutions without sounding like I’m dumping responsibility on the team?
What should I say instead of “get creative” when resources are constrained?
How do I communicate constraints and still motivate the team to explore options?
How does my framing change how teams interpret my intent?
search_terms: how to ask for creative ideas under constraints
summary: The post helps leaders improve how they communicate when asking teams to find creative solutions under constraints. It explains why “get creative” is frequently misheard as a lack of support and an offloading of responsibility, and offers a more effective framing that names constraints, makes a specific request, and signals shared ownership. It’s aimed at managers and team leads who want better alignment and motivation during tense, high-stakes situations.

title: TBM 210: Just...
questions: Why do leaders keep saying 'we just need' and make the wrong moves during complex problems?
How can teams explore messy, interlocking causes without prematurely converging on a single root cause?
What facilitation practices help shift from sensemaking to action without losing learning?
How do we prevent experiments from becoming a cure-all while still making progress?
summary: This post argues that leaders under pressure often fall into the 'justing' trap—believing a single hire, reorg, metric, or root cause will fix a complex situation—especially when they rush to converge. It is for CEOs and leaders who need a practical way to handle intractable problems by exploring the mess safely, preserving the sensemaking, running cautious experiments, and then returning to the mess to sustain learning.

title: TBM 206: When is RACI the Right Tool?
questions: When is RACI the right tool for a team or project?
What team dynamics (trust, norms, psychological safety, incentives) indicate RACI will help?
How do dependencies and negative externalities between teams affect the need for RACI?
When should leaders avoid RACI and use other mechanisms instead?
summary: The post outlines practical situations where a RACI (responsibility assignment) matrix is likely to improve collaboration by clarifying roles and ownership. It is aimed at leaders and project managers dealing with unstable teams, low trust, unclear mandates, misaligned incentives, and complex dependencies. It also implicitly contrasts RACI with alternatives, suggesting RACI is most appropriate when other support for team resilience and alignment is limited.

title: TBM 44/52: Dissonance, Diffusion, and Debt
questions: What should you do when dissonance becomes unbearable at work?
How does dissonance get transferred from one role to another in an organization?
Why does organizational “debt” feel non-urgent to individuals even when it accumulates?
What does it mean to release “stored” dissonance during transformation?
summary: The post explains how cognitive dissonance in workplaces is managed through a set of coping mechanisms and how, when thresholds are exceeded, people shift burdens, degrade work, reframe narratives, or leave. It argues that dissonance diffuses across roles and systems, making organizational “debt” hard to perceive while still accumulating. It is aimed at leaders and teams planning change or transformation who need to understand what gets “released” beneath the surface.

title: TBM 40/52: Drivers vs. Fixers
questions: What should I do when I hit a roadblock: push forward pragmatically or fix the underlying mess?
How do incentives and rewards cause drivers and fixers to reinforce each other’s worst patterns?
Why do teams get stuck in exclusion and mono-culture between operators and thinkers?
How should leaders structure teams so drivers and fixers can partner effectively?
summary: This post contrasts two organizational mindsets—drivers who optimize for near-term forward progress and fixers who focus on untangling underlying problems. It argues that when one mindset dominates, self-reinforcing loops of exclusion, stereotypes, and mono-culture emerge, harming both progress and retention. The piece is aimed at leaders and team builders who need to design incentives and team structures that let drivers and fixers partner productively.

title: TBT 35/52: The Benefits of Stubborn Consistency
questions: What do we value, and do team members clearly understand what “good” looks like?
Are our practices culturally dependent, and do we apply them consistently enough to create predictability?
Are we opinionated and coherent—or incoherent and maladaptive?
If conditions change, will we update beliefs and try new ideas with enough curiosity?
summary: The post argues that team and organizational success depends less on any single engineering practice and more on coherence, intentionality, and consistent cultural signaling. It uses a pair-programming example to show how stubborn conviction can be effective when paired with fairness and the ability to update beliefs as conditions change, and it challenges leaders to examine what they value and how they model “good.”

title: 32/52: Telephone Game!
questions: How does information get distorted as it passes through multiple people in an organization?
Why do priorities skew (e.g., important becomes code red) as messages propagate?
What happens when questions and feedback flow back upstream?
How do feedback avoidance and information forking create ambiguity for managers?
summary: The post uses “telephone game” diagrams as a metaphor for how messages, priorities, and context mutate as they travel through teams. It’s aimed at people trying to improve organizational alignment, decision quality, and feedback practices by understanding how distortion, prescriptiveness, and avoidance behaviors compound over multiple hops.

title: TBM 24/52: Your Head Hurts. But Why?
questions: Why do teams feel discomfort when the official story doesn’t match reality?
How does chronic dissonance become the norm and affect trust and performance?
What makes dissonance compound across different parts of an organization?
How can leaders talk about dissonance safely and productively without blame?
search_terms: acute vs chronic dissonance at work
summary: The post explores how cognitive dissonance shows up in everyday organizational life—through mismatched messages, unclear rules, and expectation/capacity gaps—and how it can feel physically and emotionally taxing. It’s aimed at leaders, managers, and team members who want a practical way to recognize acute vs chronic dissonance, understand how it compounds and drains energy, and discuss it more safely by focusing on lived experience rather than blame.

title: TBM 20/54: "You're Making This Too Complicated"
questions: How do I tell the difference between analysis paralysis and productive deep work?
Why do people interpret my approach as “too complicated” or “not bringing solutions”?
What should a team do when members have different action vs analysis defaults?
How can I adjust my style to be more empathetic and reduce triggers?
search_terms: how to handle “you’re making this too complicated”
summary: The post uses home organization styles as a metaphor for how people approach work—ranging from deep exploration to action-first execution. It’s aimed at team members and leaders who struggle with misreads like “analysis paralysis,” “not bringing solutions,” or “you’re making this too complicated.” The core guidance is to build self-awareness, discuss different approaches explicitly, and assume good intent to reduce trigger-driven misunderstandings.

title: TBM 30/52: What the **** Does That Even Mean?
questions: Which words in our team’s communication cause the most confusion?
Where should we focus our conversations and energy to reduce misunderstanding?
How do we decide whether a term needs shared definition, can tolerate disagreement, or should be left undefined?
What should we do with language that isn’t helpful or is actively harmful?
summary: This post offers a lightweight facilitation activity for teams to surface confusing terminology and decide how to handle it. It’s aimed at leaders and facilitators who want practical ways to align on definitions, tolerate productive disagreement, and identify words to leave undefined or stop using.

title: TBM 17/52: Decision Making vs. Decision Understanding
questions: Why do teams keep going back to the drawing board after they think they decided?
How can we reduce decision drift when uncertainty and context keep changing?
What’s the difference between decision making and decision understanding in real team work?
When do goals, OKRs, and RACI help versus when do they become harmful proxies?
summary: This post argues that team problems labeled as “decision making” are often actually problems of “decision understanding,” where shared vocabulary, assumptions, and implications fail to converge. It’s aimed at leaders and team members working on complex, cross-disciplinary work who are trying to prevent decision drift without relying solely on planning frameworks, goals, or role assignment charts.

title: TMB 12/52: Breaking the Wicked Loop
questions: How do teams end up in a wicked loop where goals don’t stick and decisions drift?
Why do new plans and reset efforts sometimes make the situation worse?
What practical interventions can restore decision stability and collaboration?
How should teams adapt during high-uncertainty periods like a pandemic?
search_terms: breaking reinforcing feedback loops in teams
summary: This post explains how teams can fall into a reinforcing wicked loop where rising fatigue and stress reduce decision quality and collaboration, which then undermines progress and decision stability. It’s aimed at leaders and team members who are stuck in repeated resets and want practical ways to break the cycle—especially by reducing pressure, working small safely, and improving uncertainty tolerance rather than constantly switching plans.

title: TBM 33/53: But Why Is It Working?
questions: Why do different organizational cultures produce similarly positive outcomes for customers and teams?
How can I tell whether a practice like DRI, OKRs, or meeting size will fit my team’s culture?
Is psychological safety universal, or does it manifest differently across environments?
How do my biases and worldview affect which work ideas I adopt?
search_terms: why organizational practices work differently by culture
opposite of Anna Karenina principle in organizations
summary: The post argues that successful organizations may be culturally very different, so practices that work in one context can fail when copied into another. It’s aimed at leaders and practitioners who want to choose tools and operating mechanisms (like DRI, OKRs, meeting norms, and psychological safety practices) based on cultural fit, supported by organizational self-awareness, bias recognition, and intentional listening.
